article_title: Pyrenees Cave at 2,235 Meters Yields 23 Hearths and Burned Green Minerals, Reframing High Mountains as Sites of Prehistoric Industry
summary: Fully-verified archaeology coverage; every radiocarbon date, specific, and quote confirmed verbatim against the primary Frontiers paper and press release. The lone automated warning is a false positive (the flagged word is inside a verbatim quote).

findings.message: Loaded-language pattern matched inside a verbatim quote (false positive)
findings.details: The detector flagged 'clearly'. It appears only inside a direct, verified quote from co-author Dr Julia Montes-Landa ('…which clearly suggests that fire played an important role in their processing…'), not in the article's own prose. No corrective action required.

content_preview.title: Pyrenees Cave at 2,235 Meters Yields 23 Hearths and Burned Green Minerals, Reframing High Mountains as Sites of Prehistoric Industry
content_preview.summary: A cave high in the eastern Pyrenees held 23 hearths and over 170 burned green mineral fragments, suggesting repeated prehistoric visits tied to early copper processing rather than passing traffic.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Archaeologists excavating a cave high in the eastern Pyrenees have documented 23 hearths and more than 170 fragments of a burned green mineral, evidence that points to repeated prehistoric occupation linked to the early processing of copper. The site, known as Cave 338, lies at an altitude of 2,235 meters above sea level, [according to the study published in Frontiers in Environmental Archaeology](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fearc.2026.1811493/full). The findings ch...

editor_notes.content_quality: Rich, carefully-hedged archaeology News piece (758 words) that distinguishes the confirmed finds from the preliminary malachite identification, with disciplined primary-source citation.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 snapshots read from disk (HTTP 200). source-0 (Frontiers in Environmental Archaeology paper) confirms Cave 338, the 2,235 m altitude, the 6 m² ('six square meters') excavation, the 170+ green-mineral fragments, the malachite/copper-carbonate preliminary identification, and every radiocarbon date (NA 3 3,464–2,532 cal BC, NA 2 1,109–908 cal BC, earlier 5,000–4,360 cal BC). source-1 (Frontiers press release) confirms the 23 hearths, the Carlos Tornero (Catalan Institute) lead, the malachite-preliminary and Montes-Landa 'thermally altered' quotes, the University of Granada affiliation, the child finger-bone/baby-tooth (~11 years), the brown-bear-tooth pendant, and the 'marginal' and 'short to medium' quotes. source-2 (ScienceDaily) confirms the ~2,000-year span and the Tornero 'two pendants' quote. source-3 (Smithsonian) confirms the thermally-altered-fragments framing.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every radiocarbon date and specific traces verbatim to the primary Frontiers paper (Rule 9), and every quote is verbatim and correctly attributed (Tornero, Montes-Landa). '6 m²' is rendered as 'six square meters' (unit spelled out). The agent correctly omitted the paper's internally inconsistent EC1–EC21 combustion-structure numbering.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and appropriately cautious; the malachite identification is repeatedly flagged as preliminary, and 'What We Don't Know' scopes the open questions.
editor_notes.originality: New topic — distinct from the recently-covered silver-mecon materials and Atom Computing quantum stories, and from the existing Stanford continental-mantle-earthquake article.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully-verified archaeology submission with model primary-source discipline. APPROVE — the single automated warning is a verified false positive.
